signals.headline: HEADLINE:** US March CPI surges to 3
signals.raw:
HEADLINE:** US March CPI surges to 3.3% year-over-year on historic 21.2% monthly gas spike tied to Iran war | YES | HIGH confidence

The March 2026 Consumer Price Index data, released April 10, 2026, showed **headline inflation jumped 0.9% monthly and 3.3% year-over-year**, driven by a 21.2% monthly surge in gasoline prices—the worst single-month gas increase since 1967[2]. Oil prices spiked 45% i
matched_markets: Will annual inflation increase by 3.8% in April?

signals.headline: **Fed rate cuts delayed:** The Federal Reserve held rates at 3
signals.raw: **Fed rate cuts delayed:** The Federal Reserve held rates at 3.50%-3.75%, with most policymakers expecting at least one 2026 rate cut, but a growing minority now sees no cuts at all[2].

signals.headline: **Core inflation contained:** Core CPI remained relatively benign at 2
signals.raw: **Core inflation contained:** Core CPI remained relatively benign at 2.6% year-over-year and 0.2% monthly, suggesting the inflation shock is primarily energy-driven rather than broad-based[1][4].

signals.headline: **Goldman's revised forecast:** Goldman Sachs raised its December 2026 headline PCE inflation forecast by a full percent
signals.raw: **Goldman's revised forecast:** Goldman Sachs raised its December 2026 headline PCE inflation forecast by a full percentage point, with most revision attributable to energy effects[4].

signals.headline: **Inflation expectations rose:** Short-term consumer inflation expectations jumped to 4
signals.raw:
**Inflation expectations rose:** Short-term consumer inflation expectations jumped to 4.8% annually in the University of Michigan survey, while financial market-implied expectations for 2026 headline CPI inflation rose 0.8 percentage points to 3.3%[4].

This data confirms that geopolitical factors—not monetary policy—are now the dominant inflation driver, fundamentally altering rate-cut timelines
